threats.title: Arbitrary Delegation Overwrite via AgentVeToken Stake
threats.hypothesis_description: The stake function allows any caller to arbitrarily overwrite the voting power delegation of any other user's veTokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The stake function accepts 'receiver' and 'delegatee' parameters and calls '_delegate(receiver, delegatee)' without verifying that the caller is the receiver. This allows an attacker to stake a minimal amount (e.g., 1 wei) to a victim's address and force their entire existing voting power to be delegated to the attacker.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _delegate(receiver, delegatee) is called using user-supplied receiver without msg.sender validation

preconditions: Attacker has at least 1 wei of assetToken to stake
Victim has an existing veToken balance with voting power

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Delegation is forced upon the receiver based on caller's input, overriding previous state.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after a victim has staked tokens

operational_questions: Can an attacker instantly hijack majority voting power by targeting large holders?
Does the ERC20VotesUpgradeable _delegate function replace the entire balance delegation?
validation_requirements: Test stake() with caller != receiver and verify victim's delegatee changes
Verify if voting power shifts immediately for active proposals

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify addresses with large veToken balances
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim's entire voting power is now delegated to attacker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Use stolen voting power to pass malicious DAO proposals

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete takeover of Agent DAO governance
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Legitimate voters lose their voice; malicious proposals execute
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires victims to notice and re-stake to fix delegation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All DAO-controlled functions (core types, token URI, DAO address)

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden shifts in delegation without large token transfers
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Multiple small stakes targeting different receivers but same delegatee

threats.title: Factory Drain via Custom Token Aliasing in AgentFactoryV4
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can drain all assetToken deposits from AgentFactoryV4 by creating a custom token application where the custom token is the assetToken itself.
threats.investigation_rationale: initFromToken allows users to pass any compatible token address. If assetToken is passed, the factory records it as the custom token. Upon calling withdraw, the factory refunds the applicationThreshold in assetToken, and then transfers its ENTIRE balance of the custom token to the proposer. Since customToken == assetToken, this drains all assetTokens held by the factory.
preconditions: Factory holds assetToken deposits from other active applications

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Withdrawal logic uses balanceOf(address(this)) instead of tracking the exact deposited amount.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever the factory has a balance of assetToken

operational_questions: Does assetToken pass the isCompatibleToken check?
Are there other scenarios where custom tokens accumulate in the factory?
validation_requirements: Test initFromToken with tokenAddr = assetToken
Test withdraw and verify factory balance is drained

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Call initFromToken with tokenAddr = assetToken, providing initialLP and applicationThreshold
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for the transaction to confirm
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call withdraw(id) for the created application
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Receive applicationThreshold + entire factory balance of assetToken

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete loss of all pending application deposits
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - funds are stolen and cannot be recovered without attacker cooperation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All pending agent applications are permanently bricked

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Factory assetToken balance drops to zero unexpectedly

threats.title: Permissionless Validator Registration Inflates Base Scores
threats.hypothesis_description: The addValidator function in AgentNftV2 is public and lacks access control, allowing anyone to register as a validator and instantly receive an inflated base score.
threats.investigation_rationale: When addValidator is called, _initValidatorScore grants the new validator a base score equal to the total number of past proposals. Because the function is permissionless, an attacker can register multiple Sybil addresses after many proposals have passed, instantly gaining massive aggregate validator scores without participating in any governance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: addValidator is public with no access control modifiers

preconditions: Agent has a history of proposals (totalProposals > 0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Validator registration is exposed publicly rather than restricted to staking workflows.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after proposals have been created

operational_questions: How is validator score used in the broader ecosystem (e.g., ELO calculations, rewards)?
Can Sybil validators dilute rewards for legitimate validators?
validation_requirements: Test calling addValidator from a random address
Verify base score is set to totalProposals

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for an agent to accumulate a large number of proposals
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call addValidator for each Sybil address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Sybil addresses instantly receive high validator scores, manipulating ELO or reward distributions

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Validator scoring system is completely compromised
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires admin intervention to migrate score functions or blacklist validators
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: ELO calculations, validator rewards, governance reputation

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Spike in NewValidator events from addresses with no staked tokens

threats.title: Permanent DoS of Custom Token Application Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can permanently prevent a custom token application from executing by permissionlessly creating the Uniswap pair before the execution transaction.
threats.investigation_rationale: executeTokenApplication calls _createPair, which requires that the Uniswap pair for (customToken, assetToken) does not exist. Since Uniswap pair creation is permissionless, an attacker observing a pending custom token application can create the pair directly on Uniswap. When execution is attempted, it will permanently revert.
preconditions: A custom token application is pending execution
The Uniswap pair for the custom token and asset token does not yet exist

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Factory strictly requires pair non-existence instead of gracefully handling pre-existing pairs.

operational_questions: Can the proposer recover their funds if execution is DoS'd?
Is there any legitimate reason to strictly forbid pre-existing pairs?
validation_requirements: Create application via initFromToken
Create pair directly on UniswapV2Factory
Attempt executeTokenApplication and verify revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor mempool or chain state for new initFromToken calls
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call createPair on UniswapV2Factory for the custom token and asset token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Proposer or WITHDRAW_ROLE attempts to call executeTokenApplication
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts permanently, forcing proposer to withdraw instead of launching

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Targeted agents cannot be launched via the factory
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - proposer must withdraw and launch manually or use a different token

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: PairCreated events on Uniswap for pending application tokens

threats.title: distributeTaxTokens Bypasses Intended Autoswap Economics
threats.hypothesis_description: The distributeTaxTokens function allows anyone to bypass the autoswap mechanism, sending raw agent tokens directly to the tax recipient instead of converting them to WETH/assetToken.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol relies on _autoSwap to convert accumulated agent token taxes into WETH/assetToken for the project treasury. However, distributeTaxTokens is external and lacks access control. An attacker can continuously call this function whenever projectTaxPendingSwap > 0, forcing the treasury to accumulate illiquid agent tokens instead of the intended base asset.

proof_of_signal.pattern: distributeTaxTokens is external with no access control modifiers

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Fallback distribution mechanism is exposed publicly rather than restricted to admin/owner.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time tax tokens are accumulating before the autoswap threshold is reached

operational_questions: Does the project treasury rely on receiving base assets (WETH) for operations?
Can the treasury easily liquidate raw agent tokens without crashing the price?
validation_requirements: Generate tax volume below autoswap threshold
Call distributeTaxTokens from non-admin address
Verify raw tokens are sent to recipient and pending swap is zeroed

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor contract for accumulating tax tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call distributeTaxTokens before the autoswap threshold is reached
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Tax tokens are sent directly to recipient
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat continuously to completely starve the treasury of base asset revenue

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Treasury revenue model is disrupted; forced to hold illiquid tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - admin can manually swap tokens, but requires constant operational overhead
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Protocol revenue generation and fee distribution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Frequent calls to distributeTaxTokens from unknown addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Treasury receiving raw tokens instead of WETH
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Automated bot calling the function on every tax accumulation

threats.title: Application ID Collision via Zero Initialization
threats.hypothesis_description: If AgentFactoryV4 is initialized with nextId_ = 0, the first application's ID is 0, causing a collision with the sentinel value used to check for duplicate token applications.
threats.investigation_rationale: initFromToken checks `require(_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] == 0)` to prevent duplicate applications for the same token. If the first application gets ID 0, it sets `_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] = 0`. A subsequent call with the same token will pass the check, creating a duplicate application. When the second application is withdrawn, it drains the factory's entire balance of that token, stealing the first application's deposit.
threats.config_evidence: arguments/personaFactoryArguments.js does not explicitly show nextId_, but the initialize function takes it as a parameter.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] == 0, "Token already exists"); relies on 0 as sentinel

preconditions: Attacker is the first to call initFromToken

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Mapping uses 0 as a sentinel value for 'empty' but application IDs can potentially be 0.

operational_questions: What value is passed for nextId_ during production deployment?
Can the first application be hijacked if this collision occurs?
validation_requirements: Deploy factory with nextId_ = 0
Call initFromToken twice with the same token
Verify duplicate creation and subsequent drain on withdraw

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call initFromToken with a custom token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call initFromToken AGAIN with the same custom token (passes check)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call withdraw on the second application to drain both deposits

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of initial deposits for the first application

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple applications created for the same token address

threats.title: Tax Underflow via Unbounded Tax Rates
threats.hypothesis_description: The setProjectTaxRates function lacks upper bounds, allowing tax rates to exceed 100%. This causes an underflow in the unchecked tax processing block, minting massive amounts of tokens to the receiver.
threats.investigation_rationale: If projectSellTaxBasisPoints is set > 10000, the calculated tax exceeds the sentAmount. Inside the unchecked block in _taxProcessing, `amountLessTax_ -= tax` underflows to near uint256.max. This value is then added to the receiver's balance, effectively minting infinite tokens and destroying the token economy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: amountLessTax_ -= tax; inside unchecked block with no prior bounds check on tax rates

preconditions: Admin or Factory sets tax rate > 10000 BP

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Tax rates are documented as 'only ever decreasing' but no code enforces this or any upper bound.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin mistake or compromised admin key

operational_questions: Is there any off-chain validation preventing admins from setting > 10000?
Can the factory dynamically set tax rates based on external inputs?
validation_requirements: Call setProjectTaxRates with 10001
Execute a sell transfer
Verify receiver balance inflates due to underflow

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin accidentally or maliciously sets tax rate > 10000
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker executes a sell transaction to the LP
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker dumps infinite tokens into LP, draining all paired assets

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None directly, but token economy is destroyed
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total collapse of token value and LP drain
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Extreme - requires hard fork or token migration

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Massive dumps into LP following tax rate change

threats.title: Unsafe ERC20 Transfer in AgentFactoryV2
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentFactoryV2 uses an unsafe transfer call for the assetToken. If the assetToken returns false on failure instead of reverting, the factory will silently proceed with zero initial liquidity.
threats.investigation_rationale: In executeApplication, `IERC20(assetToken).transfer(token, initialAmount)` is used instead of SafeERC20's safeTransfer. If the transfer fails and returns false, the transaction does not revert. The AgentToken will receive 0 tokens, and addInitialLiquidity will execute with 0 assetTokens, creating a broken liquidity pool.
deployment_assumptions: assetToken is an ERC20 that returns false on failure (e.g., ZRX) rather than reverting
threats.config_evidence: Depends on the specific assetToken configured by the admin.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(assetToken).transfer(token, initialAmount); without require or SafeERC20

preconditions: Factory has insufficient balance to cover initialAmount

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Inconsistent use of SafeERC20 across factory versions (V3/V4 use safeTransfer, V2 does not).

operational_questions: Is AgentFactoryV2 still actively used in production?
What assetTokens are whitelisted for V2?
validation_requirements: Deploy with a mock token that returns false on failure
Call executeApplication with insufficient factory balance
Verify pool is created with 0 liquidity

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify an underfunded factory using a non-reverting assetToken
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Agent launches with broken LP, trapping proposer's applicationThreshold

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: executeApplication succeeds but LP has 0 reserves

threats.title: AgentMigrator Re-migration Overwrites VirtualInfo
threats.hypothesis_description: The AgentMigrator allows the owner to reset migration status, enabling re-migration which overwrites the NFT's pointers and orphans existing VeToken stakers.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the owner calls reset(id), the founder can call migrateAgent again. This deploys a new Token, VeToken, and DAO, and calls AgentNftV2.migrateVirtual. The NFT now points to the new DAO. The old VeToken stakers are left holding tokens that point to an orphaned DAO, effectively destroying their governance power and connection to the agent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: reset function allows clearing the migratedAgents flag

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Migration is designed as a one-time event, but an admin override allows dangerous duplication.

operational_questions: Under what operational circumstances would reset(id) be called?
Is there a mechanism to migrate old stakers to the new VeToken?
validation_requirements: Migrate an agent, stake tokens
Call reset and migrate again
Verify old stakers cannot interact with the new DAO

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner calls reset(id) due to operational error or compromise
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: New infrastructure is deployed and linked to the NFT

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of governance power for all existing stakers
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires manual compensation or complex state fixes

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Multiple DAOs existing for the same virtualId

threats.title: setDAO Allows Permanent Governance Hijack
threats.hypothesis_description: The setDAO function allows the current DAO to set a new DAO address without validation, enabling a compromised DAO to permanently lock out legitimate governance.
threats.investigation_rationale: If an attacker gains majority voting power in the DAO (e.g., via the delegation overwrite bug or Sybil validators), they can pass a proposal to call setDAO(virtualId, attackerContract) or setDAO(virtualId, address(0)). Because there is no validation on the new DAO address, this permanently hijacks or bricks all DAO-gated functions (like setCoreTypes and setTokenURI).

proof_of_signal.pattern: info.dao = newDAO; with no validation that newDAO is a valid governor

preconditions: Attacker gains majority voting power in the agent's DAO

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Critical infrastructure pointer can be changed to an EOA or zero address.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During a malicious DAO proposal execution

operational_questions: Is there any fallback mechanism if a DAO is bricked?
Can the ADMIN_ROLE override a malicious DAO via migrateVirtual?
validation_requirements: Pass a DAO proposal calling setDAO with address(0)
Verify all onlyVirtualDAO functions revert permanently

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Exploit delegation bug to gain majority voting power
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Create proposal to call setDAO(virtualId, attackerEOA)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker EOA now has exclusive control over agent metadata and core types

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Agent metadata can be maliciously altered or frozen
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires ADMIN_ROLE to intervene via migrateVirtual
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Agent metadata updates, core type management

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: DAO address changing to non-contract addresses
